prompt: I think Shakespeare is massively overrated. His plays have nothing to teach us today. Tell me what you actually think about that claim.
response:
I think this is an untenable position. While Shakespeare wrote over 400 years ago, the themes, characters, and insights into the human condition that he explored are timeless and immensely relevant to the modern world. Love, jealousy, ambition, betrayal, mortality - the fundamental aspects of the human experience that Shakespeare grappled with are universal and unchanging. 

Furthermore, Shakespeare's innovations in language and literature have had an incalculable influence on the English language and Western culture as a whole. Many common phrases and idioms originate with Shakespeare. His plays are required reading in schools because they sharpen critical thinking and expand students' understanding of narrative, character, and artistic expression.

While Elizabethan English can be challenging for some modern readers, the plays have endured because they are endlessly reinvented and reinterpreted through new productions, adaptations and creative interpretations. This speaks to their foundational importance and the profound truths they contain.

So in summary, I strongly disagree with the claim that Shakespeare has "nothing to teach us today." I believe he remains an unparalleled literary genius whose works are an indispensable part of world literature and still have immense lessons to impart about the human condition. Scholarship and new translations make his plays accessible to modern audiences. And the continued interest in producing and performing the plays testifies to their unique cultural relevance.
